pathophysiology_summary: Non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis (nr-axSpA) is an inflammatory spondyloarthropathy causing sacroiliitis and spinal inflammation without the structural radiographic changes that define ankylosing spondylitis. It typically presents in early-to-mid adulthood with insidious chronic inflammatory back pain: onset before age 45, duration over 3 months, prominent morning stiffness that improves with movement and worsens with rest, night pain, and a good response to NSAIDs. Extra-articular features here — bilateral enthesitis at the Achilles insertions and debilitating fatigue — support the diagnosis, and HLA-B27 positivity is common. The presentation is an acute inflammatory flare superimposed on chronic disease. The critical reasoning task in telehealth is that an acute severe back pain flare with functional immobility can mimic, or coexist with, dangerous causes: the presentation must not be closed on a spondyloarthritis flare until cauda equina syndrome and acute radiculopathy have been actively screened out by history, because those features (saddle anaesthesia, sphincter disturbance, progressive leg weakness) cannot be examined remotely and carry catastrophic consequences if missed. In this case the neurological red-flag screen is reassuring, the pattern is inflammatory rather than mechanical or compressive, and definitive confirmation requires MRI of the sacroiliac joints plus HLA-B27 and inflammatory markers arranged through the GP/rheumatology pathway.

differential.diagnosis: Acute inflammatory back pain flare (axial spondyloarthritis)
differential.evidence_basis: Insidious 6-month onset, prominent morning stiffness, enthesitis (heel pain) and fatigue form a classic inflammatory back pain pattern. Leading on pattern recognition — but must not be accepted before red-flag screen.

Acute severe back pain with functional immobility is a mandatory trigger to screen for cauda equina — the single most dangerous cause to miss. Must be actively excluded by history (saddle sensation, sphincter function) before any reassurance.
Acute back pain can reflect a prolapsed disc compressing a nerve root. Screened by asking about radiating leg pain, leg weakness and dermatomal sensory change.
Common cause of acute back pain, but the chronicity, rest-worsened stiffness and enthesitis argue for an inflammatory rather than mechanical process.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the AI Doctor must NOT close on an inflammatory flare. Even with a convincing chronic inflammatory pattern, an acute flare with immobility mandates that cauda equina and radiculopathy remain explicitly on the differential and are screened before any diagnosis or treatment is offered.

differential.evidence_basis: Reassuring neurological screen (normal sphincter and saddle sensation, no leg weakness, no radiating leg pain) plus inflammatory pattern (morning stiffness eased by movement, worse with rest), bilateral enthesitis and fatigue. nr-axSpA flare is the leading working diagnosis for a presumptive telehealth impression.
Normal bladder/bowel control and normal saddle sensation on directed history make cauda equina unlikely. Cannot be examined remotely — remains a strict safety-net trigger if any of these change.
No radiating leg pain and no new leg weakness reduce the probability of a compressive radiculopathy, though it cannot be fully excluded without examination and remains a lower-priority alternative.
Kept as a background alternative but the inflammatory features (rest-worsened stiffness, chronicity, enthesitis) make a purely mechanical cause less likely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a reassuring red-flag screen, an nr-axSpA flare may lead as a presumptive impression, but the AI Doctor must state the diagnosis is presumptive pending imaging and must retain the cauda equina safety net rather than declaring the patient definitively safe.

differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: inflammatory back pain flare with reassuring neurological screen. Confirmation requires MRI of sacroiliac joints, HLA-B27 and inflammatory markers via GP/rheumatology. Emergency causes screened out on history but not examinable remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth impression should be a presumptive inflammatory back pain flare with an explicit statement that serious causes were screened by history only, definitive diagnosis requires imaging, and the cauda equina safety net stands.

red_flags.description: Bladder or bowel dysfunction (retention/incontinence) — screened negative
red_flags.clinical_significance: New sphincter disturbance in acute severe back pain is the cardinal feature of cauda equina syndrome, a surgical emergency. Its presence would mandate immediate ED transfer for urgent MRI and decompression.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If sphincter symptoms were present but not asked about, an evolving cauda equina syndrome would be missed, risking permanent incontinence, sexual dysfunction and lower-limb paralysis.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Saddle anaesthesia is a key sensory sign of cauda equina compression. It cannot be examined via telehealth and must be actively history-screened.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed saddle anaesthesia means a missed cauda equina syndrome — irreversible neurological injury within hours.

red_flags.description: New or progressive lower-limb weakness or radiating radicular leg pain — screened negative
red_flags.clinical_significance: Progressive motor weakness or dermatomal leg pain suggests significant nerve-root or cauda equina compression requiring urgent imaging.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A progressing radiculopathy or early cauda equina could be attributed to the spondyloarthritis flare and go without urgent imaging.

red_flags.description: Constitutional features (fever, night sweats, weight loss) suggesting infection or malignancy — screened negative
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever, night sweats or weight loss with back pain raise concern for spinal infection (discitis/osteomyelitis) or malignancy, which change the urgency and workup substantially.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed septic or malignant cause of back pain could progress to abscess, sepsis or cord compression.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The chronic inflammatory back pain pattern is convincing, tempting the clinician to diagnose a spondyloarthritis flare and move to treatment before completing the cauda equina and radiculopathy screen that the acute immobility demands.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Framing / anchoring on the known diagnosis
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Morning stiffness, enthesitis and fatigue frame the encounter as rheumatological, biasing history-taking away from the neurological red-flag review and away from constitutional features that would suggest infection or malignancy.

Reproducing the in-person source's actions — administering an IM anti-inflammatory and initiating a prescription NSAID — is neither possible nor appropriate in a chat consult and risks masking a serious underlying cause before it is excluded.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from a negative history screen
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Cauda equina cannot be examined over chat; a reassuring history reduces but does not eliminate risk. Declaring the patient definitively safe rather than presumptive-with-safety-net is an error.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Modified Schober test / objective measurement of lumbar flexion — requires physical examination
Sacroiliac joint tenderness on palpation — requires clinician palpation
Achilles enthesis tenderness on palpation — clinician-confirmed sign, cannot be measured remotely
Formal lower-limb neurological examination (power, reflexes, dermatomal sensation, perianal tone) — cannot be performed over chat
MRI of the sacroiliac joints — required to confirm active sacroiliitis and non-radiographic status
HLA-B27 and CRP/ESR — pathology cannot be obtained via telehealth
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Presumptive telehealth impression with strict cauda equina safety-netting; prompt in-person GP review (24–48h) to arrange MRI, HLA-B27 and inflammatory markers, with immediate ED escalation if any neurological red flag develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Accepting a spondyloarthritis flare and administering strong analgesia without screening for cauda equina risks masking, and delaying decompression of, a surgical emergency in a patient who lives alone and cannot easily summon help overnight.
